name: Memory-Mapped I/O
def: Mapping a file or device into the address space so it is accessed as memory.
problem: Access large files lazily and share them between processes without explicit read/write syscalls.
mechanism: mmap maps file pages into virtual memory; the OS demand-pages on access and writes back dirty pages, with the page cache as the buffer.
benefits: Lazy demand paging of huge files
Simple pointer access to file data
failures: SIGBUS when the underlying file shrinks
Thrashing when the mapping exceeds RAM
whenNot: Small files; streaming sequential I/O with simple semantics
myths: "mmap is always faster than read" — for sequential streaming, buffered read can win
